did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DeepCNN) for the detection and segmentation of skin cancer. The model is optimized using a proposed Fractional Gannet Humming Optimization (FGHO) algorithm, which integrates Fractional Calculus, Gannet Optimization Algorithm (GOA), and Artificial Hummingbird Algorithm (AHA). The study involves image pre-processing, segmentation using an encoder-decoder with Dense-Residual blocks, feature extraction (CLBP, GLCM, LVP, SLBP, CNN features), and classification/detection of skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents a novel deep learning technique (FGHO_DeepCNN) for skin cancer detection and segmentation. It details original experimental work including image pre-processing, segmentation using an encoder-decoder with Dense-Residual blocks, feature extraction, and classification. The study reports specific experimental outcomes and performance metrics (PPV, NPV, TPR, TNR, accuracy) on multiple datasets, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Fractional gannet humming optimization enabled deep convolutional neural network for detection and segmentation of skin cancer
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel Fractional Gannet Humming Optimization enabled Deep Convolutional Neural Network (FGHO_DeepCNN) for the detection and segmentation of skin cancer. The methodology involves image pre-processing using a bilateral filter, lesion segmentation via an encoder-decoder with Dense-Residual blocks optimized by the Fractional Gannet Optimization Algorithm (FGOA), and feature extraction using CLBP, GLCM, LVP, SLBP, and CNN features. The classification task is performed by a DeepCNN tuned with the proposed FGHO algorithm, which integrates Fractional Calculus, Gannet Optimization, and the Artificial Hummingbird Algorithm. The model was evaluated on multiple datasets, achieving an accuracy of 93.40% on the first dataset, 90.67% on dataset 2, and 90.15% on dataset 3. The results indicate that the FGHO_DeepCNN approach attains superior performance in terms of PPV, NPV, TPR, and TNR compared to other techniques. The authors conclude that this approach holds practical significance for widespread use in dermatology clinics and hospitals for skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on the detection and segmentation of skin cancer. The abstract and introduction describe skin cancer as a disorder caused by unchecked proliferation of aberrant skin cells leading to malignant tumours. The study does not specify distinct histological subtypes (e.g., melanoma, BCC, SCC) but treats 'skin cancer' as the general diagnostic class for the classification task.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion regions in input images
all_task_types.details: The paper performs skin lesion segmentation using an encoder-decoder with Dense-Residual blocks (DRB) trained by the Fractional Gannet optimization algorithm (FGOA).

The paper performs skin cancer detection/classification using a Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DeepCNN) tuned by the Fractional Gannet Humming Optimization (FGHO). The task involves categorizing skin cancer, with performance metrics including accuracy, PPV, NPV, TPR, and TNR reported for multiple datasets.

conclusions: The proposed Fractional Gannet Humming Optimization enabled Deep Convolutional Neural Network (FGHO_DeepCNN) achieved superior performance in skin cancer detection and segmentation compared to other techniques.

On Dataset 1, the FGHO_DeepCNN model attained a Positive Predictive Value (PPV) of 89.80%, Negative Predictive Value (NPV) of 89.40%, True Positive Rate (TPR) of 94.50%, True Negative Rate (TNR) of 94.00%, and accuracy of 93.40%.
On Dataset 2, the model achieved a PPV of 91.68%, NPV of 88.46%, TPR of 91.68%, TNR of 91.23%, and accuracy of 90.67%.
On Dataset 3, the model obtained a PPV of 90.56%, NPV of 90.36%, TPR of 90.95%, TNR of 90.87%, and accuracy of 90.15%.
The study concludes that the FGHO_DeepCNN approach holds practical significance for widespread use in dermatology clinics and hospitals for skin cancer detection.
important_remarks: The specific sources, names, and sizes of the three datasets used for evaluation are not identified in the provided text, preventing assessment of data leakage risks or population bias.
The study does not specify distinct histological subtypes of skin cancer (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma), treating 'skin cancer' as a general binary class, which limits clinical granularity.
No statistical hypothesis testing or confidence intervals were reported; performance claims rely solely on point estimates of accuracy, PPV, NPV, TPR, and TNR.
The paper claims 'practical significance' and widespread use in clinics, but provides no evidence of external validation, clinical trials, or deployment in real-world settings.

The methodology combines multiple complex optimization algorithms (Fractional Calculus, Gannet Optimization, Artificial Hummingbird Algorithm) with deep learning, introducing potential complexity and reproducibility challenges without ablation studies in the provided context.
